subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

files.fileName: To what extent do western beauty standards influence eating disorders in teens-10.pdf

strengths: The title and introduction clearly communicate the research topic and rationale.
Purpose and scope remain visible throughout the report, showing consistent focus.
weaknesses: Research question is too broad and only partly connected to each disorder in the discussion.
Methodology lacks detailed search strategy, screening process, and critical appraisal protocols.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the topic is communicated effectively (band 5â6 descriptor), the research question is overly broad (band 3â4) and the methodology is only partially developed (band 3). Balancing these, a holistic score of 2/6 reflects clear communication but significant gaps in focus and method.

strengths: Definitions of disorders are clear and supported by prevalence data.
Good synthesis of multiple studies tracing the evolution of Western beauty ideals.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on secondary websites and popular articles instead of peer-reviewed sources.
Frequent spelling, grammar, and citation inconsistencies reduce professionalism and precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good conceptual understanding and some synthesis (band 3â4) but inconsistent source quality and terminology errors limit it to a holistic score of 2/6.

strengths: Inclusion of over twenty academic sources, including relevant meta-analyses.
Recommendations for future research are concrete and well aligned with identified gaps.
weaknesses: Analysis remains surface-level with unsubstantiated causal claims and no systematic critical appraisal.
Evaluation is basic and non-critical, lacking counter-evidence and depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research relevance is good (band 4â6), but analysis and evaluation are limited (band 1â3). A balanced holistic score of 3/12 acknowledges solid source selection but shallow critical thinking.

strengths: A clear, logical structure with all required IA headings and a comprehensive table of contents.
weaknesses: Inconsistent in-text citation formatting and erratic bibliographic style.
Figures lack captions and source details; page numbers and layout conventions are uneven.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure is appropriate (band 3â4), but numerous formatting and layout errors (band 1â2) warrant a holistic score of 1/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological terms (e.g., âbody dissatisfaction,â âthin ideal,â âbinge episodesâ) are used and usually defined, showing basic fluency.  Nonetheless, spelling and grammatical slips (e.g., âthinnes,â âAFRIDâ) and occasional misuse of technical vocabulary reduce consistency.  This places the work comfortably in the âmostly accurateâ 3-4 band rather than the consistently accurate 5-6 band.

Basic formatting conventions are met: title, candidate details, word count and a table of contents are provided.  However, in-text citations are inconsistently formatted, page numbers are not visible in the extract, image captions lack full source details, and bibliographic style is erratic.  These shortcomings correspond to the lower band âsome layout considerations missing or incorrect.â

The student demonstrates reasonable conceptual understanding of anorexia nervosa, BED, and ARFID, and cites several peer-reviewed studies (e.g., Stice & Shaw 2002; Grilo 2009).  However, many references are secondary websites (Mayo Clinic, KidsHealth) or popular science articles, and several citations are incomplete.  Source material is summarised more than synthesised, but overall accuracy is generally sound, fitting the âgood knowledge with mostly relevant source applicationâ band.

The methodology consists of a brief paragraph outlining inclusion criteria (publication date, relevance, sampling size, transparency) and the use of Google Scholar/ChatGPT Scholar for locating sources.  No systematic search strategy, data-extraction table, or critical appraisal protocol is provided, and there is no primary data collection.  Nevertheless, some evidence of informed source selection and ethical awareness is shown.  This aligns with the âmostly complete methodology with generally relevant sources/methodsâ descriptor, justifying a 3.

The research topic â the impact of Western beauty standards on three specified eating disorders in adolescent females â is stated explicitly in both the title and the introduction.  The introduction explains why the issue is important, identifies the target population, and previews the structure of the investigation.  While phrasing is occasionally awkward, the purpose and scope are unmistakable and remain visible throughout the body of the report, satisfying the descriptor for clear and appropriate communication.  Minor language lapses prevent it from feeling fully âexcellent,â so the lower end of the 5-6 band is selected.

Roughly twenty academic sources are referenced, including several meta-analyses that are clearly pertinent to the RQ, indicating a reasonable level of research relevance.  However, the inclusion of non-scholarly websites dilutes overall quality, and source credibility is not critically appraised.  These factors are characteristic of the lower end of the 4-6 band (âgood research mostly relevantâ).

The research question appears verbatim as the title and is re-stated in the introduction, showing basic clarity.  However, including three separate disorders under one umbrella makes the question rather broad, and later sections treat each disorder in only a cursory way.  Links back to the RQ are present but sometimes implicit rather than explicit.  This places the work in the âclearly stated but only partially focused and connectedâ level, meriting the mid-band score.

A conventional structure (title page, table of contents, introduction, literature review, discussion, conclusion, bibliography) is present and logically ordered, satisfying all required IA headings.  Minor inconsistencies (e.g., some headings duplicated, slight section mislabelling) prevent awarding the maximum 4, but overall the structure is clearly appropriate.

The discussion section summarises earlier points and briefly acknowledges other variables (genetics, peer pressure), showing some attempt at evaluation, but the reasoning is not systematically developed and counter-evidence is absent.  Arguments remain broad and descriptive rather than critical.  Consequently, the strand fits the 1-3 âbasic argument with non-critical evaluationâ category.

The student largely paraphrases study findings and then asserts a causal link between beauty standards and each disorder without demonstrating how competing explanations or methodological limitations affect the conclusions.  Analysis therefore remains surface-level, with only occasional attempt to weigh evidence.  This limited depth aligns with the mid-range of the 1-3 band.

annotations.comment: The title clearly communicates the focused research question and scope, establishing a strong foundation for the investigation.
Word count is provided and below the 4000-word limit, demonstrating awareness of IB requirements.
Indent sub-headings more clearly in the TOC to differentiate primary and secondary levels of structure.
Page numbers are not shown in the footer; add visible numbers on each page to satisfy formatting requirements.
Source attribution for the 1970s incidence rate is vague; include full citation details inline for clarity.
Page number is not visible on this page; include numbering in the footer to meet formatting conventions.
The table of contents is comprehensive and logically orders sections and sub-sections.
The introduction succinctly presents the research focus and rationale, situating the topic within contemporary trends.
The figure is presented without a caption or source details; add a descriptive caption and reference.
Expand the methodology section to detail search keywords, databases queried, and screening process for source selection.
Mentioning ethical use of AI is commendable, but elaborate on potential biases introduced by AI-driven tools.
The criteria list demonstrates some methodological planning, but lacks details on how reliability and bias were assessed.
Spelling errors such as âvarieringâ and âthinnesâ disrupt professionalism; proofread technical vocabulary.
Definitions of the three disorders are concise but contain grammatical errors and lack nuance; refine language and expand clinical detail.
Introduce a critical appraisal tool (e.g., CASP) to systematically evaluate study quality and manage bias.
Historical overview provides context but lacks citations for Renaissance and early 20th-century claims.
Good synthesis of multiple studies illustrating the evolution of Western beauty ideals over time.
The sentence âThis constant flashlight on these norms can psychological impact young womenâ is unclear; revise syntax for clarity.
Consider critiquing methodological limitations of the TikTok and Instagram studies to deepen evaluation.
Definitions of anorexia nervosa are clear and backed by prevalence data, aiding reader understanding.
The image lacks a caption and integration in text; explain its purpose and source.
Citation formatting is inconsistent in this section; standardize use of parentheses and italics.
The discussion of Fardouly et al. (2015) effectively links social media use to body concerns among adolescents.
Provide a comparative prevalence table for the three disorders to strengthen analytical depth.
The NIMH prevalence statistic lacks an in-text author-year citation; insert parenthetical reference.
The argument linking ARFID to beauty standards is speculative; seek direct empirical studies on this relationship.
Recommendations for future research are well-aligned with identified gaps, proposing concrete interventions.
The ARFID description distinguishes it well from other disorders, enhancing conceptual clarity.
Prevalence data from Norris et al. (2016) is presented passively; critically assess sample representativeness.
In the discussion of Stice & Shaw (2002), include sample size and design details to bolster evidence credibility.
Overstates causality in discussion by claiming a directly causal link; revise to reflect correlational nature of evidence.
Bibliography entries are inconsistent in style; adopt one citation format and apply it uniformly.
Several duplicate or partial entries are present in the bibliography; remove redundancies and complete missing elements.
The bibliographic â761 wordsâ entry appears in the bibliography section rather than with the cover page; reposition accordingly.
